sentences.text: We're talking about the episodes?
When I was in Warsaw, I got a job as a manager of a fruit and vegetable wholesale.
At first I became a manager of a store, and then of a wholesale business.
While I was a manager at the store there used to come a woman-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a store for Germans only.
She was a very pretty woman with a red head.
I knew that she was Jewish.
I was trying to talk to her to get her confident, but I couldn't.
She-- she was afraid of me, I think.
But nevertheless, she spoke to me quite often.
Whenever-- she was working for some German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- as a housekeeper.
I knew that she was not a housekeeper.
She was very intelligent, witty, fast.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after the Warsaw uprising-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe I should-- I met her after the Warsaw uprising, but I have to go back to the uprising.
I'm talking about the uprising in the Polish section of Warsaw.
That occurred in 1944.
Well, yes, I was in Warsaw.
No, no, we-- when we left Alston, we left for-- for good.
We never went back to the town Alston.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in Warsaw.
And while in Warsaw, I met that woman.
Now, after the Warsaw uprising in the Polish, we-- we went to the-- to the uprising.
We were hiding.
We-- The Germans were burning out the houses.
Every house, house by house.
And I was-- as a matter of fact, they were put our house on fire, and I was trying to get the fire out with a bucket.
They were handing me buckets of water, but I couldn't manage it.
They started to shoot, and the ricochet-- that was the street where they were shooting-- the bullets were ricocheting.
I got scared.
I knew I'm going to get hit sooner or later, so I got out.
A woman took my place.
But anyway, we went back and we-- my wife and I went behind our-- the complex of the houses.
They were the former Polish Ministerium of Agriculture, which-- which was burnt in 1939 when the war started.
And my wife and I, we-- we found a hole.
But it was a very narrow hole that you could only slide in with your leg.
Somebody evidently dug a hole in there.
So we were up in that hole, and I covered up the-- the-- the opening with an empty cement bag.
And that's where we spent two days with my wife while the fighting was going on.
One morning, we heard the Germans, and one said, raus, or I'll throw a grenade.
So I said to my wife, that's it.
I got out, but I noticed that that fellow with the grenade [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had an accent.
It was a very-- the people from Rhineland, where I was born, have a very slight accent.
Slight, very difficult to-- but I knew he was from there.
And I asked him, are you from Rhineland?
He said, ja, was machst du hier?
Yes, he said, what are you doing here?
So I told him that I am a German businessman that has been caught in the Warsaw uprising.
And my wife was smart enough, she destroyed my ID card, so there we was, again, without papers.
And so he didn't shoot me.
They took us to an underground of the Polish opera.
There were probably 200 or 300 women up there in the basement.
And one-- one day came the SS and, [GERMAN].
And there were probably not too many men-- few men only.
And the moment they got out, they shot them.
But my wife had a fur coat, and she put a fur coat on me, on top of me, and sat on me.
And there was a German soldier, a young SS man.
He was going with this rifle in his arm, patrolling.
He was singing, Mommy, give me a-- a horse.
Horse is my paradise.
And he said to my wife, [GERMAN], which means, it's always time to go to heaven.
That young fellow, he wasn't more than 20 years old.
He saw it, but he didn't say anything.
And then, one day, came a major SS.
Since there were so many bodies laying around, he had a handkerchief in his mouth like that because he didn't want to inhale the-- the stench.
And he asked me, was-- what are you doing out here?
So again, I told him my story-- that I was caught, I was a businessman, and-- and so that was it.
He didn't say anything.
Next day, he came with another fellow who was adjutant.
You know what adjutant means?
A very arrogant fellow with a Russian name.
And he started to interrogate me.
He was suspicious.
At one moment, he asked me, what kind of ID card did I have.
I didn't have an idea [INAUDIBLE] what kind of ID card the German had, but I remember one I saw, ID card with a blue stripe.
I told him, with a blue stripe.
And he saw-- told to that major, he's not a German.
But he didn't say anything.
But that major was also from Rhineland.
Two days later, he came again, and he said, are you still here?
I said yes, I have no way to get out.
And he told me, I'm not going to take you on my back out.
Then I knew that I would probably be saved, because he was friendly, you know.
He took-- so they took us from there.
They took us to the Polish-- former Polish Ministerium of Foreign Affairs, where they had a hospital for the wounded.

I have to mention that the battalion that was operating in the-- earlier where we were caught, that was the center of the city, was a [NON-ENGLISH], which meant they had all the SS men that have been outlaws that were imprisoned.
They sent them to rehabilitate themselves.
So they were the worst cutthroats ever.
It's even written about it in books.
And they had a hospital up there, and my wife was working in the kitchen, and I was helping up there, something-- I don't know what, but anyhow.
And one day, I saw a German SS man.
He was a tall fellow.
I know he was cross eyed.
He had two baskets full of crystals, like that.
And I looked at him, and he looked at me, and he said to me, you [GERMAN].
You Polish pig, he said to me.
And I told [INAUDIBLE], I said, excuse me, I'm not a Pole, I said.
I'm a German.
Oh, [INAUDIBLE], I apologize, and so on.
And he said, what I'm doing.
I said that I'm trying to get out.

So he told me next day at 4:00-- you know, they were stealing all the stuff wherever they could, and they make packages, and every day they sent a truck with packages out of Warsaw where they went to the post office in [INAUDIBLE], a town near Brno, and sent them to Germany, to their families.
Excuse me.
And so it was.
Next day, they came and took us to Warsaw-- out of Warsaw, yes.
The fighting was still going on.
They told us that just at one particular moment, they let us know.
Then we have to duck, because there was a sniper from the underground, from the Polish army.
They will shoot.
And so it was.
When the truck made a turn, it was the time that it went up a little bit.
And then, when the fellow is shooting, he could hear the truck coming, he had his rifle set.
Anyhow, the shot came-- two shots, about one foot above our head.
We went to a town, [?
Belani-- ?] that is suburb of Warsaw-- and we went to the priest for-- for-- to get a sleep.
Those fellows had their quarters, the Germans.
But the priest would not accept us.
They all were afraid.
You know?
They're all afraid.
But we went to the church and we slept in the church.
Then when they cut [INAUDIBLE], they put the caskets and we slept there one night.
And next day, one of the Germans said, the best way for you be if you take a train from here to Cracow, he said to us.
He-- he knew that was-- something was not kosher.
And so we left them, and we came to a place where the-- the-- the employees of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] city of Warsaw had a kind of convalescent house.
But we didn't know it, but at the same house was a hospital for the Polish underground.
We didn't know about it.
They didn't tell us.
But one day-- we found out that one day some German doctors came.
They were looking for a place to establish a hospital, and then we find out.
They told me to hold up the Germans while they were taking out all the-- the-- in the back, in the woods, the wounded ones.
And we were there for a few weeks.
Then I became a translator for somebody, for the agriculture inspector.
And then, later on, I established my own fruit and vegetable wholesale, which became a large wholesale.
The situation was right.
Nobody lived in Warsaw anymore.
But all the agricultural goods were, around Warsaw, still there.
It was in August, 1944.
It was at this time that that woman I told you, the redhead, saw me on the street.
She came running to me, put her arms around me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally, she trusted me.
And I said-- but she still didn't tell me what they were.
Told me that she's over here with her friends, this and this cousin, [INAUDIBLE] nothing was true, and they don't know what to do, if I could help them.
So I said, OK, send them over to me, and I gave them a job loading cabbage-- whatever it was-- because I was selling about 15 car loads of vegetables a day.
We sold 4,000 tons a month.
It was the largest-- we employed 117 people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then one day, I-- I knew that two Jews, you know, were working over there, you know.
People start to talk.
And I said, I have room for one fellow in the office.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I asked, what are-- is your profession?
The one fellow said, I am a merchant.
The other said, I am doctor of philosophy.
I know a doctor of philosophy wouldn't-- wouldn't load cabbage.
So the one fellow said-- he suggested-- he said, the doctor of philosophy, he's not as strong as I am.
Maybe you take him to the office.
So I took him to the office.
And when the Russian came in, he said, Mr.
Damski, my name is Dr.
Ordova. ?] He was a professor of the University in Lvov.
And that red head was his wife, and the other woman was his sister, and the man was his cousin, whatever he was.
I don't know.
So in this respect, I helped another four Jews to survive.
So--
Well, there were others, too, but some of them never-- never-- never called me anymore after war.
I-- there were some that I help, I give them money, even.
I know he was a doctor and his wife was a doctor.
They both were doctor.
When the war was out, they never even sent me a letter.
But, that's people.
And then, at the time of-- town of [INAUDIBLE], when the Russian came in, and that was it.
The war was over.
Yes, but I want to go to Zamosc, where my wife's parents lived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had a very large storage room.
As a matter of fact, I had about over 100 ton of vegetables in that storage-- 110 tons, exactly.
And there was a Russian soldier with a [INAUDIBLE] machine gun, and I introduced myself as the owner of this house-- of this business.
I would like to get some sacks-- sacks to get my stuff out.
Not too much, two or three sacks.
And he said to me in Russian, [RUSSIAN], which means in Polish-- in English, not allowed.
You have to see the captain.
So I figure, I'm going to see the captain, he's going to lock me out-- up.
As a matter of fact, they were trying to lock me up later on, but I wasn't there anymore.
The Russians.
Not the Germans anymore.
So we-- Because they thought you were a German?
Well, I was a capitalist.
I was a capitalist.
You know, I was the-- German.
Everybody that-- that had shovel, in a sense, was helping the Germans.
But could not be avoided.
The way to survive was, the best way, if you were working for a German company, because you could be arrested any time in Poland.
In Warsaw, they made a-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH], which means they surrounded two or three blocks, and arrested everybody they got hold of, and sent to either-- most of the time, to labor camps.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's how we survived the war.
My brothers-- they both died.
They both died a few years ago.
My younger sister still lives in Warsaw, and she's married and has a son.
That my sisters in Warsaw.
One sister was killed by the Russian bomb.
That was when-- in 1945 already, when the Russians were advancing and they were supposed to bomb a railroad station in town [INAUDIBLE], what was a [INAUDIBLE] station.
But they didn't hit the station.
You know, if-- if someone doesn't know the Russian, he doesn't really know they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russian is a different people than we are.
Everything is what they say.
They don't care about a thing.
So, we dropped the bomb-- he dropped the bomb.
Don't really matter if you hit it or not.
I spoke to-- to a Russian, [NON-ENGLISH] which means a hero of the Soviet Republic.
He was [INAUDIBLE] twice a hero of the Soviet Republic.
It's the highest-- highest honor a Russian can get.
And I-- and he had a watch that was given to him by Roosevelt, and he told me it was given when he was bombing Berlin.
So I ask him, it must be very difficult to bomb Berlin.
Oh no, he said, it was not, he said.
[RUSSIAN].
We had a drink a little bit, and off we went.
See, that is the attitude.
A drunken pilot cannot hit the target.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, I have a photograph with three Russian soldiers.
Two of them are heroes of the Soviet-- Soviet Republic.
This is from right after--
Right after the war, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Russians are different people.
They-- they steal everything.
They sell everything.
When we opened a business with my father-in-law, we needed CO2 gas for the-- for the seltzer water and so on.
But we didn't have enough gas.
I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ordered it from a journal.
It came every day, four bottles of seltzer water.
So one day he told me, [NON-ENGLISH] which means a very strong water, a lot of gas.
So I said, well, we don't have any gas, I told him.
Oh, he said [NON-ENGLISH], we have.
He said, oh yeah.
We always say the Russian always have everything, up to here.
And he said, I will bring you.
So next day, he brought me a big bottle of 40 kilograms, and I test it.
It was-- it was real.
I said, can you bring me anymore?
He said, sure.
[INAUDIBLE] So I give him exactly a liter of vodka.
Few days later, there came a truck-- a big truck-- loaded with that CO2.
They sold me everything they had.
And a month later, I found out that they were looking all over the town, what happened to it.
They stole it.
They were using it for cooling the gasoline, the fuel in the-- in the-- in airplanes.
And they stole everything they had, and sold it to me, the Russian.
[LAUGHTER] Now, so going-- about the Russians, they are also very antisemitic.
I was going in supper, in a town, and there were about five Russian soldiers, drunk, and hollering, [RUSSIAN], which means, we cut out every Jew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the real Russian when he gets drunk.
My father was captured by the Russians, and he died in a labor camp.
He was in a uniform, yes.
And so they captured him and sent him to labor camp, and he died.
I-- we never got any letter from my father, but I went to a doctor in [INAUDIBLE], and he said to me, Damski, this name is familiar.
That's the first word I heard from my father.
So he told me how my father looked like.
Yeah, that's my father.
But my name is not so common name.
Very few families only have that name.
And he said my father was in a prison in Kiev, and from there they send him out to a camp.
Either to a camp of, you know, where they shot people, or to a labor camp.
I don't know.
Never found out.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Poles?
No.
No.
I knew of one girl that lived with a Polish fellow, but I never met him.
She told me about him, but I never met him.
So he was also helping sometimes.
No, I didn't help.
Oh, he help her.
Yeah, he was living with her.
She lives in Israel.
She wanted to marry him, but her family wouldn't let her.
It was a big tragedy.
After liberation-- after liberation, I was in business with my father-in-law.
We had representation of some breweries and soft drinks.
And-- but then the county co-operative took it over, after we established the people-- the business, brought it back to life.
They asked my father-in-law to become a manager, but he refused.
And he then-- later on, he got out of Poland and went to Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I was just-- I had my own little business, but not very successful.
But I saw that I have no place in Poland.
And I have to say that my wife was the-- was the force behind me to do everything to get out of Poland.
I couldn't-- could never own business.
I would never become a member of the party.
That was out of the question, like it was out of the question to become a German.
And we had a little money, and we spent all the money to obtain passports.
The first difficulty was to get the visa, but because I was born in Germany, I came to the United States on the German visa.
German quota, pardon me.
Because the German quota was very, very large, but very few people-- very few Germans in Poland that could immigrate to the United States.
So I was lucky.
I got the-- and that visa didn't cost me anything, but to get the passport from the Polish government, that was a thing.
That took all the money we had.
As a matter of fact, we payed $1,000 for a passport.
That was an enormous amount of money, since a fellow that was working for the city or government made about $5 a month at this time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I was able to emigrate to the United States.
I was first-- we went to Detroit.
I was working for General Motors, first at the assembly line, then as an electrician.
Then I became a supervisor in the electrical department for a-- for a plant.
And from then, I came over to California because my wife wanted to go to California, and I think she was right.
[LAUGHS] So I started to work at Lockheed.
I was supposed to work for General Dynamics, but somehow I didn't like it.
You know, they were so strict.
It was at the time when they developed the Atlas missile.
Everybody-- the guards running with revolvers, you know, and as a foreigner, you feel very, very strange.
Because you had to have a clearance from the Navy, clearance from the Air Forces, and I just didn't feel, you know-- feel free.
But when I came to Lockheed, I was working for missiles-- missiles and space division.
It was the same situation, but more relaxed.
And I was there for five years.
After five years, they moved to Sunnyvale, the whole department.
But we just bought this house.
We couldn't afford to move there.
And I-- I got a call from Kodak company.
I don't know who-- who recommended me.
I never found out.
They wanted me to work for them.
So I was working for them for three and a half months.
Then I got a call from my former superior.
He was working in Skunk Works at Lockheed, which means the most-- you know the Skunk Works?
Yeah.
But then they-- I couldn't work at the Skunk Works because I had relatives-- I even had to give the name of the relatives in Israel.
It was so strict.
But they told me I shouldn't feel bad, because they have about 200 Americans that they could not let work at the Skunk Works.
So then I was working with the airplanes, and I was very happy with them.
It was a very good company to work for.
A very good relation with all my friends.
And then I retired in 1980.
And after 1980, I was working for [?
Ashkenazi ?] Galleries as an art consultant, and that's it.
But now I'm doing nothing and everything.
Optimist?
Always.
Definitely.
I always believe that I somehow will survive.
When I was in prison, I-- somebody gave me a razor blade, I mean a-- from a small one.
I was-- we were not allowed to look through the windows, but I was standing behind, and with my one hand I was cutting, with that little blade, the bar.
I was about three quarters through.
We had a plan to escape, two of us.
And when I was three quarters, they sent me a floor below.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, yes.
I managed-- well, it took me-- I don't know.
It took probably two or three months.
But I managed to make that slot [INAUDIBLE] that much more.
One day they sent us to this floor below.
